---
title: "Introduction to PXDataset2 object"
author: "Mohieddin Jafari"
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')`"
output:
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
---
## Resources
Bioconductor packages tagged by "proteomics", n = 177 on Nov2024, n = 188 on 8th Apr2026:
https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/BiocViews.html#___Proteomics

```{r setup, include=TRUE, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE, message=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
## install.package("BiocManager")

# library(BiocManager)
# 
# # Bioconductor packages
# if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
#     install.packages("BiocManager")
# 
# BiocManager::install("tidyverse")
# BiocManager::install("factoextra")
# BiocManager::install("msdata")
# BiocManager::install("mzR")
# BiocManager::install("rhdf5")
# BiocManager::install("rpx")
# BiocManager::install("MsCoreUtils")
# BiocManager::install("QFeatures")
# BiocManager::install("Spectra")
# BiocManager::install("ProtGenerics")
# BiocManager::install("PSMatch")
# BiocManager::install("pheatmap")
# BiocManager::install("limma")
# BiocManager::install("MSnID")
# BiocManager::install("rawrr")
# BiocManager::install("RforMassSpectrometry/SpectraVis")
# 
# install.packages("tidyverse")
```

## Review some points about MS data
### Dynamic range of signals

**Dynamic range** describes the span between the smallest and largest signal a system can detect or measure reliably.  
It is usually expressed in **orders of magnitude (powers of 10)**.

>For example:
- A dynamic range of $10^{6}$ means the largest signal is **1,000,000× bigger** than the smallest detectable one.
- In biology, dynamic range is critical because molecules can exist at **vastly different concentrations in the same sample**.

![Mount Damavand](fig_script/damavand.jpeg){fig-align="center" width=75%}
![Fruit fly](fig_script/drosoph1c.jpg){fig-align="center" width=25%}


- Mount Damavand: $\sim 5.6 \times 10^{3}$ m  
- Fruit fly: $\sim 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m  
- Ratio: $\sim 10^{6}$ (6 orders of magnitude)

---

### Detection technologies

| System / Technology | Dynamic range (orders) | Molecular coverage | Physical scale (meters / concentration) | Damavand analogy |
|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------|
| Human eye (adapted) | $\sim 10^{9}$–$10^{12}$ | Macroscopic scene | Brightness: $\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{4}$ cd/m² | From sunlight to starlight (time-adapted) |
| Mass spectrometry | $\sim 10^{4}$–$10^{6}$ | $\sim 10^{3}$–$10^{5}$ proteins | $\sim 10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}$ M (pM–µM) | Damavand ($5.6 \times 10^{3}$ m) → fruit fly ($3 \times 10^{-3}$ m) |
| General chemical assays (UV, fluorescence, NMR) | $\sim 10^{3}$–$10^{5}$ | 1–10 analytes | $\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ M (µM–mM) | Damavand → building scale |
| Biological proteome (human plasma) | $\sim 10^{10}$–$10^{12}$ | $\sim 10^{4}$–$10^{5}$ proteins | $\sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-15}$ M (mM–fM) | Damavand → sub-viral / molecular scale |

![](fig_script/Coverage.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}

### Signal representation in MS

- The continuous workflow of separating-separating-separating!

- Three dimensions: Retention time of analyser elution - Mass to charge (M/Z) - Intensity

**Spectrum is the unit of mass spectrometry data**


*TIC (total ion chromatogram) plot*: The intensities of all ions in each mass spectrometer scan (within a given time period) are summed and plotted as a function of time.
*Base peak chromatogram plot*: The intensity of the base peak (the most intense ion in each mass spectrum) is plotted as a function of time (Note that the base peak should not be confused with the baseline, which represents the constant background signal of the instrument).
*XIC or EIC plot*: The intensity of a specific analyte at a chosen m/z value (either total or base peak intensity) is isolated and plotted as a function of time.

![Chromatogram plots](fig_script/TIC_XIC.png){fig-align="center" width=70%}

## Group Activity

Check this file "https://github.com/jafarilab/MSProt2026/blob/main/GroupActivity_20Apr.qmd".

## Getting data
Get to know the ProteomeXchange Consortium: Established to create globally coordinated, standardized pipelines for data submission and dissemination across major proteomics repositories, the Consortium promotes open data policies in proteomics.

`proteomexchange.org`

```{r, message=T, eval=FALSE}
#Direct accessing data
library(rpx)
library(tidyr)
px <- PXDataset("PXD022816")
pxget(px, grep("mzID", pxfiles(px))[1:3]) # It might take time for the local access  
pxget(px, grep("mzML", pxfiles(px))[1:3]) # It might take time for the local access 

(px <- PXDataset("PXD000001")) # To remove any dataset from cache use:unlink(px@cachepath, recursive = TRUE) 
(pxfiles(px)) # file names of the object

fn <- "TMT_Erwinia_1uLSike_Top10HCD_isol2_45stepped_60min_01-20141210.mzML"
mzf <- pxget(px, fn)

pxtax(px) # taxonomic name
pxref(px) # bibliography ref



pxget(px, "erwinia_carotovora.fasta")
pxget(px, "README.txt") # a vector defining the files to be found, try to find it on your device.
# Some files are accessible remotely and some of them from local cashing mechanism.
pxfiles(px) %>% str() # To check 

(f <- pxget(px, "TMT_Erwinia_1uLSike_Top10HCD_isol2_45stepped_60min_01-20141210.mzML"))
basename(f)

```

## MS example data: msdata
```{r}
# Various Mass Spectrometry raw data example files for proteomics and metabolomics
library(msdata) 
proteomics()
ident()
quant()
```
### Quiz 1 (GA_4)
Visit `gosocrative.com` and enter room name `FIN2026`

## Raw MS data
- We need to convert binary-vendor-specific formats to open formats such as mzML, mzXML.
- mzML is the most recent format.
- proteowizard msconvert: https://proteowizard.sourceforge.io/ or search in Google msconvert GUI
- ThermoRawFileParser: https://github.com/compomics/ThermoRawFileParser

- To check all MS-related data format:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mass_spectrometry_data_format

## MS data from R prospective

>What is the difference between DDA and DIA from a data acquisition perspective?

![Three dimensions in DDA](fig_script/DDA_example1.png){fig-align="left" width=50%}![Three dimensions in DDA](fig_script/DDA_example2.png){fig-align="rigth" width=50%}
![Three dimensions in DIA](fig_script/DIA_example.png){fig-align="center" width=50%}


![How it looks in R](fig_script/raw.png){fig-align="center"}
